coredata.dc:title: Selective dual-mode detection of doxycycline using orange carbon dots/calcium-murexide composite system: From spectroscopic analysis to smartphone-based sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdel-aal, Fatma A.M.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Rageh, Azza H.
Elmasry, Mohamed R.
Jardan, Yousef A. Bin
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: A novel and selective analytical method has been developed for doxycycline (DOX) determination, addressing the critical need for monitoring this widely used antibiotic in environmental samples. The method employs a hybrid sensing system combining orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) with a calcium-murexide (Ca@Mu) complex, offering a unique dual-mode detection approach. Unlike conventional methods that rely on direct fluorescence quenching by DOX, which often suffer from poor selectivity, this system utilizes the competitive binding between DOX and murexide for Ca2+ ions. Initially, the pink-colored Ca@Mu complex formed under alkaline conditions causes fluorescence quenching of O-CDs at 552 nm. Upon introduction of DOX, its stronger Ca2+-binding affinity leads to the formation of a calcium-DOX complex, liberating free Mu and triggering both colorimetric (pink to orange) and fluorometric (restoration of O-CDs emission) responses. Comprehensive characterization and mechanistic investigations employing different spectroscopic techniques confirmed the sensing mechanism. The method demonstrates excellent selectivity for DOX among other tetracyclines and potential interferents, along with impressive analytical performance including good linearity (1.0-35.0 μM), low detection limit (325 nM), and high precision. Additionally, a smartphone-based colorimetric platform was developed for convenient on-site analysis. The practical utility of this method was validated through successful determination of DOX in environmental water samples with excellent recovery rates, offering a reliable and user-friendly approach for environmental monitoring of this important antibiotic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doxycycline
Dual-mode sensing
Competitive binding
Smartphone-based detection
Water analysis

Analysis of DOX in real water samples
Structural and morphological studies of O-CDs
Determination of DOX via smartphone platform
Determination of DOX in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: size distribution histogram), (B) XRD pattern, (C) XPS spectrum, and (D) FTIR spectra of O-CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence and excitation spectra of O-CDs, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of O-CDs after exposing to different excitation wavelengths ranging from 400 to 500 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of O-CDs/Ca@Mu in the DOX concentration range 0 – 35.0 μM. (B) The linear relationship between the (F-F0) and the DOX concentration (1.0 – 35.0 μM), where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity after and before addition of DOX, respectively. All experiments were done in triplicate and error bars indicate SD of triplicate measurements.

A linear relationship between DOX concentration (10.0 – 40.0 μM) and (a) value under the irradiation of visible light (Inset: the images of the O-CDs/Ca@Mu with different concentrations of DOX under visible light). The (a) value, which indicates the position on the green-red axis in color space. All experiments were done in triplicate and error bars indicate SD of triplicate measurements.
Graphical presentation of detection mechanism of DOX.

(A) Emission spectra of O-CDs, O-CDs/Ca@Mu, and O-CDs/Ca@Mu + DOX, (B) Spectral overlay of excitation and emission spectrum of O-CDs and UV visible absorption spectra of Ca@Mu, and (C) UV/Vis absorption spectral of Ca@Mu, DOX, and Ca@Mu + DOX.
Comparison of the proposed method with other reported fluorometric methods for DOX detection.

Tap water and
simulated biological system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the analysis of DOX in real water samples (n=3).

head.ce:title: Selective dual-mode detection of doxycycline using orange carbon dots/calcium-murexide composite system: From spectroscopic analysis to smartphone-based sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and selective analytical method has been developed for doxycycline (DOX) determination, addressing the critical need for monitoring this widely used antibiotic in environmental samples. The method employs a hybrid sensing system combining orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) with a calcium-murexide (Ca@Mu) complex, offering a unique dual-mode detection approach. Unlike conventional methods that rely on direct fluorescence quenching by DOX, which often suffer from poor selectivity, this system utilizes the competitive binding between DOX and murexide for Ca2+ ions. Initially, the pink-colored Ca@Mu complex formed under alkaline conditions causes fluorescence quenching of O-CDs at 552 nm. Upon introduction of DOX, its stronger Ca2+-binding affinity leads to the formation of a calcium-DOX complex, liberating free Mu and triggering both colorimetric (pink to orange) and fluorometric (restoration of O-CDs emission) responses. Comprehensive characterization and mechanistic investigations employing different spectroscopic techniques confirmed the sensing mechanism. The method demonstrates excellent selectivity for DOX among other tetracyclines and potential interferents, along with impressive analytical performance including good linearity (1.0-35.0 μM), low detection limit (325 nM), and high precision. Additionally, a smartphone-based colorimetric platform was developed for convenient on-site analysis. The practical utility of this method was validated through successful determination of DOX in environmental water samples with excellent recovery rates, offering a reliable and user-friendly approach for environmental monitoring of this important antibiotic.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxycycline (DOX), a second-generation tetracycline antibiotic derived from oxytetracycline, is widely prescribed for treating bacterial infections, including respiratory tract infections, skin conditions, and sexually transmitted diseases [1]. Additionally, it serves as a prophylactic agent against malaria and is increasingly utilized in veterinary medicine for livestock treatment [2]. Global consumption of DOX has reached significant levels, with annual usage estimated at several thousand tons, raising concerns about its environmental impact and potential health risks. Prolonged exposure to DOX can lead to various adverse effects, including photosensitivity, gastrointestinal disturbances, and the development of bacterial resistance [3,4]. Due to its high solubility and stability, DOX exhibits substantial environmental persistence, being detected in various matrices including surface waters, and wastewater effluents [5]. Compared to other tetracyclines, DOX demonstrates enhanced lipophilicity, better tissue penetration, and higher oral bioavailability (95% vs 75-80% for traditional tetracyclines), primarily due to its unique structural modification at C-6 (a methyl group) and C-5 (a hydroxyl group), leading to improved pharmacokinetic properties and broader therapeutic applications [6].

Several analytical methods have been developed for the detection of DOX in various matrices, each featuring distinct principles and limitations. Ultraviolet-visible spectrometry (UV-VIS) offers a straightforward approach for DOX detection by measuring its absorbance [7]; however, it lacks specificity due to potential interference from other substances with similar absorbance profiles. Capillary electrophoresis (CE) provides high resolution and requires minimal sample volumes, but it can be limited by complex sample preparation steps and lower sensitivity compared to other techniques [8]. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is widely used for DOX detection because of its accuracy and reproducibility; however it often requires time-consuming sample preparation procedures and expensive solvents [9,10]. Electrochemical analysis is sensitive and offers the advantage of miniaturization for on-site testing, but electrode fouling and matrix interference can limit its effectiveness [11,12]. Immunoassay-based methods provide high specificity for DOX detection with relatively simple procedures, although cross-reactivity with structurally similar compounds may affect accuracy [13]. Chemiluminescence-based methods for DOX detection are highly sensitive and rapid, but they are often complex to operate and may suffer from stability issues of the chemiluminescent reagents [14].

Fluorescent methods using nanomaterials, such as carbon dots (CDs), have emerged as powerful tools for detecting various analytes across different matrices due to their unique optical properties, stability, and biocompatibility [15]. They exhibit strong fluorescence, tunable emission wavelengths, and low toxicity, making them ideal candidate for the use in environmental, biological, and food sample analysis [16–18]. CDs can be engineered with specific functional groups to selectively bind the target analytes, enhancing their sensitivity and specificity in complex matrices. CDs have been successfully employed to detect heavy metals, pesticides, and antibiotics in water, soil, and food samples, where they respond with a measurable fluorescence change upon interaction with the analyte [19,20]. This versatility makes CDs a promising platform for developing cost-effective, highly sensitive, and portable fluorescence-based detection systems for a wide range of applications in environmental monitoring, food safety, and medical diagnostics [21,22].

Traditional CDs-based detection methods for tetracyclines (TC) primarily rely on inner filter effect (IFE), where TCs' absorption bands (200-400 nm, with peaks at 260 and 355 nm) overlap with CDs' fluorescence spectra, causing concentration-dependent quenching [23,24]. However, the similar structural and optical properties of TCs make their differentiation challenging using CDs fluorescence alone. Several strategies have emerged to address this limitation, including mathematical approaches using linear discriminant analysis and support vector machine with dual-channel fluorescence sensors [25], metal-ion assisted methods employing Al(III) with dual-emission CDs [26], and specialized CD synthesis using tobacco-derived precursors [27]. While these methods successfully differentiate various TCs, they present certain drawbacks including the need for expertise in mathematical tools and the difficulty in acquiring CDs that can independently discriminate TCs. To overcome these limitations, this work introduces a simple alternative using a hybrid system that combines CDs with Ca2+-murexide (Ca@Mu) complex, enabling specific differentiation of DOX from other TCs through unique optical properties and complex color changes.

Metal-ion murexide (M@Mu) complexes have been previously employed as analytical tools for different analytes through competitive binding mechanisms. In these systems, the analyte competes with murexide (Mu) for binding to the metal ions, leading to detectable changes in the complex's properties. Notable examples include the detection of histidine [28] and thiols [29], each utilizing different detection principles. Histidine determination relies on colorimetric changes in the M@Mu complex upon competitive binding. In contrast, thiol detection employs a fluorometric approach based on changes in the fluorescence emission of the Hg@Mu complex at approximately 450 nm. However, the fluorometric method for thiol detection has limitations, particularly its susceptibility to matrix interference, which can affect the accuracy and reliability of the measurements. This matrix interference issue highlights the need for developing more selective and robust detection methods using M@Mu complexes.

In this work, we present a novel and versatile sensing platform for DOX determination based on the interaction between orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) and calcium-murexide (Ca@Mu) complex. The distinctive feature of this system lies in its dual-signal response mechanism, where the initial Ca@Mu complex causes spectral overlap with the O-CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Upon introduction of DOX, its stronger binding affinity for Ca2+ ions leads to competitive displacement of Mu from the complex, triggering both a visible color change and fluorescence restoration of the O-CDs. This unique mechanism not only enables sensitive fluorometric detection but also allows for convenient smartphone-based and colorimetric determination of DOX using the same sensing system. The practical utility of this method was successfully demonstrated through the analysis of DOX in environmental water samples, establishing its potential for routine analytical applications.

Doxycycline (DOX), sodium hydroxide, phosphoric acid, murexide, glacial acetic acid, polyethylene glycol (PEG) monostearate, boric acid, o-phenylene diamine (OPD), ethanol, and calcium chloride were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Arginine (ARG), azithromycin (AZM), tetracycline (TC), chloramphenicol (CHL), chlortetracycline (CTC), cysteine (CYS), enrofloxacin (ENR), gentamicin (GEN), glucose (GLU), glutamic acid (GLA), glutathione (GSH), histidine (HIS), oxytetracycline (OTC), penicillin (PEN), serine (SER), and tryptophan (TRP) were supplied from alpha chemika Co., India. Aluminum chloride, magnesium chloride, ferric chloride, zinc sulphate, copper sulphate, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, cadmium chloride, and chromium carbonate were procured from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. Double distilled water (18.2 MΩ.cm) was used in whole experiments.
All specifics regarding the instrumentation used can be found in the Supplementary Data file.

The O-CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method combining two primary precursors: PEG monostearate (0.7 g) and OPD (0.1 g) in 50 mL of double distilled water. After brief ultrasonication to ensure thorough mixing, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for thermal treatment at 200°C for 6-hours. Once the reaction mixture cooled naturally to room temperature, it underwent sequential purification steps. The crude product was first passed through a 0.22 μm filter to eliminate larger particles, then subjected to dialysis using a 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off membrane for 24 hours to remove unreacted starting materials and small molecular byproducts. The purified product was then converted to solid form through lyophilization and preserved at 4°C until needed for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yields measurement of O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For a comprehensive explanation of the procedures and calculations, refer to the supplementary data file.

The detection of DOX was accomplished by preparing a reaction mixture in a 10 mL volumetric flask, combining 1.0 mL of O-CDs solution with 250 μL of 1 mM Mu, 1.0 mL of calcium chloride solution (500 μg mL-1), and 1.0 mL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 10.0). Various concentrations of DOX solution (0.5 mL) were then introduced to the mixture, and the volume was adjusted to the mark using ethanol. After a brief incubation period of 1 minute, the fluorescence measurements were taken by exciting the sample at 450 nm and recording the emission at 552 nm. A control solution was prepared following the identical procedure but omitting the DOX addition, serving as a blank for the measurements.

A smartphone-based analytical procedure was developed to complement the spectroscopic DOX detection method. The analysis was performed using DOX solutions at concentrations between 10.0 and 40.0 μM under visible light conditions. Images of the sample solutions were captured using a smartphone camera. To ensure reproducible imaging conditions, a standardized setup was established using a white bulb lamp as the light source, positioned at a fixed distance of 10 cm from the samples. Digital color analysis was then performed on the captured images using a specialized mobile application (Color detector app, Version 3.2.3) to quantify the color changes associated with varying DOX concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DOX in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: River water samples from the Ibrahimiya Canal in Assiut, Egypt and tap water from our laboratory were subjected to a systematic pretreatment protocol. First, the pH of water samples was adjusted to 10.0 to facilitate the precipitation of interfering metal ions. The samples were then allowed to stand for 30 minutes to ensure complete precipitation. Subsequently, the samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove both suspended particulates and precipitated metal hydroxides. The pretreated samples were then spiked with DOX standard solutions to achieve final concentrations of 1.0, 5.0, and 10.0 μM for recovery studies. These samples were analyzed using the established fluorescence detection method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological studies of O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The O-CDs were characterized for their size, shape, and morphology using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1A presents the TEM images, with the particle size distribution displayed in the inset, showing that the O-CDs are approximately spherical and have an average diameter of 4.3 nm. A zoom-out view illustrating the distribution of O-CDs is presented in Fig. S1, confirming their uniform dispersion throughout the sample. As shown in Fig. 1B, the XRD pattern of the O-CDs exhibits a broad peak near 17.4°, indicating the presence of graphitic carbon (001) crystal planes within the O-CDs [30]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further investigate the functional groups and elemental composition on the surface of the O-CDs. In the XPS full spectra, peaks observed at 285.50 eV, 401.00 eV, and 532.20 eV correspond to the C1s, N1s, and O1s elements, respectively (Fig. 1C). The carbon peaks at 285.07 eV, 285.71 eV, and 288.75 eV indicate the presence of C-C/C-H bonds, C-N bonds, and C=O bonds, respectively (Fig. S2A) [31]. The nitrogen peaks at 400.41 eV and 401.23 eV correspond to N-C bonds and amine/quaternary nitrogen species, confirming the successful doping of nitrogen atoms (Fig. S2B). The oxygen peaks at 532.11 eV and 532.78 eV can be attributed to C-O-C and C=O bonds (Fig. S2C) [31], likely originating from the PEG monostearate precursor.

The FTIR spectrum of the O-CDs reveals a complex molecular structure that reflects the combined spectral characteristics of its precursors, OPD and PEG (Fig. 1D). Compared to the pristine precursors, the carbon dots show characteristic transformations: the broad peak at 3172 cm⁻¹ represents N-H and O-H stretching vibrations, which is consistent with the amine groups of OPD and the hydroxyl terminals of PEG, but with enhanced intermolecular interactions during dot formation [32,33]. The small peak at 2748 cm⁻¹ for C-H stretching likely originates from the long hydrocarbon chain of PEG monostearate, maintaining a spectral signature from the original precursor. The strong absorption at 1712 cm⁻¹ (C=O stretching) indicates oxidative modifications during synthesis, diverging from the pristine precursor spectra, while the 1653 cm⁻¹ peak (C=C/C=N stretching) reflects the aromatic ring system of OPD and potential condensation reactions [34]. The small peaks at 1339 cm⁻¹ (C-N stretching) and 1179 cm⁻¹ (C-O stretching) further validate the hybrid organic structure, showing chemical transformations from the original OPD and PEG molecular frameworks. The out-of-plane vibrations at 755 cm⁻¹ and 631 cm⁻¹, attributed to aromatic C-H bending and N-H wagging, confirm the successful structural integration of both precursors into the carbon dot architecture, demonstrating a sophisticated molecular reorganization during the synthetic process.

The spectroscopic characterization of the synthesized O-CDs revealed distinct absorption and emission properties. The UV-visible spectrum exhibited two characteristic peaks: one at 258 nm, attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp² domains within the carbon core, and another at 421 nm, corresponding to n-π* transitions of surface functional groups containing C=O, and C=N bonds (Fig. 2A ) [35]. The fluorescence analysis demonstrated a prominent emission peak at 552 nm when excited at 450 nm, indicating the presence of surface-state emission due to the recombination of surface-trapped excitons [36]. The difference between the UV absorption peak (421 nm) and the optimal excitation wavelength (450 nm) in carbon dots arises from their complex electronic structure. Notably, the O-CDs displayed excitation-independent behavior, where varying the excitation wavelength from 400 to 500 nm did not shift the emission peak position but only altered its intensity (Fig. 2B), with maximum emission intensity observed at 450 nm excitation.

Regarding the excitation-independent behavior, this phenomenon occurs because the emission in our O-CDs primarily originates from a specific set of surface states with relatively uniform energy levels. When excited across different wavelengths within the 400-500 nm range, electrons are promoted to various excited states but rapidly relax to the same emissive surface states before fluorescence occurs. This energy funneling to common emissive traps results in consistent emission wavelength regardless of excitation wavelength, with only the intensity varying due to different absorption efficiencies at each excitation wavelength. The relatively high quantum yield of 23.5% suggests the formation of high-quality CDs with efficient radiative recombination pathways.

The stability characteristics of the synthesized O-CDs were evaluated under various physicochemical conditions. The fluorescence response remained notably stable across a wide range of salt concentrations (0.01-2.0 M NaCl), indicating robust emission properties even in high ionic strength environments (Fig. S3A). The photostability assessment, conducted through continuous UV exposure (365 nm) for 300 minutes, showed minimal fluorescence deterioration, demonstrating adequate resistance to photobleaching (Fig. S3B). Investigation of pH effects revealed optimal fluorescence performance in slightly acidic to basic conditions, with maximum intensity observed starting from pH 5.0 (Fig. S3C), suggesting potential sensitivity to acidic environments. Temperature-dependent studies indicated consistent fluorescence behavior between 20°C and 80°C, though significant changes were noted beyond 80°C (Fig. S3D). These comprehensive stability tests demonstrate that the prepared O-CDs maintain their fluorescence properties under moderate temperature conditions and varying ionic strengths, while performing best in neutral to alkaline media.

The development of the DOX fluorescence detection method found optimal performance at pH 10.0. Testing across pH values from 4 to 11 showed how acidity and basicity affected the stability and reactivity of the colored Ca@Mu complex (Fig. S4A). Under acidic conditions, the complex tended to break apart, while alkaline environments promoted its formation. A pH of 10.0 proved ideal as it kept the pink complex stable with maximum spectral overlap with O-CD excitation, while still enabling the DOX reaction that releases free Mu. This pH optimized the color change between states, enhancing detection sensitivity. Analysis of binding kinetics showed that just 1.0 minute of contact time was adequate for DOX complexation, though tests were conducted up to 15 minutes (Fig. S4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DOX via smartphone platform
ce:section.ce:para:
A portable smartphone-based detection platform was developed for field monitoring of DOX using the O-CDs/Ca@Mu sensing system. The system exploits the distinct color transitions observed when the sensing complex interacts with varying DOX concentrations under visible light. This smartphone-based colorimetric approach offers superior quantitative analysis compared to simple visual observation. The analytical procedure involves smartphone imaging of the detection system, followed by digital color analysis using a color detector (Version 3.2.3). The introduction of increasing DOX concentrations to the O-CDs/Ca@Mu solution triggers a gradual color transition from pink to orange (Fig. 4
inset). This unique color development can be explained by the interplay between the purple-colored free murexide and the yellow Ca@DOX complex, which are complementary colors on the color wheel, resulting in the observed orange color. The system demonstrated a consistent color evolution across DOX concentrations ranging from 10.0 to 40.0 μM under visible light illumination. The (a) value, which indicates the position on the green-red axis in color space, showed good linear correlation with DOX concentration (Fig. 4), following the equation a = 0.26[DOX] – 1.45 (R² = 0.9912). As DOX concentration increases, the restoration of orange emission causes a positive shift in (a) values, indicating movement toward the red region of the spectrum. The (a) parameter was chosen as the analytical signal due to its strong correlation with DOX levels. Using this smartphone-based colorimetric approach, a detection limit of 2.34 μM was achieved for DOX quantification.

To evaluate the performance of the sensor in environmental water samples, the responses of common ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, and Cu2+), amino acids (ARG, CYS, GLA, HIS, SER, and TRP), natural organic macromolecules (GLU and GSH), and other antibiotics (AZM, CHL, TC, ENR, GEN, OTC, and PEN) were measured with a concentration of 100 μM. As shown in Fig. S5A and Fig. S5B, these substances caused negligible interference in the O-CDs/Ca@Mu system, only the addition of DOX induced a significant response. The exceptional selectivity of the O-CDs/Ca@Mu system for DOX can be attributed to several key factors. First, the strategic selection of excitation and emission wavelengths (450 nm and 550 nm, respectively) plays a crucial role in minimizing interference. These wavelengths fall outside the absorption/emission ranges of common ionic species, amino acids, proteins, and other antibiotics present in environmental samples. This spectral isolation effectively eliminates the possibility of false positives from matrix fluorescence or spectral overlap with potential interferants. Regarding Ca2+ binding affinity, DOX demonstrates significantly stronger complexation with Ca2+ ions compared to TC and oxytetracycline due to its unique structural features. The presence of a hydroxyl group at C-5 position combined with the C-6 methyl substitution in DOX creates an optimal spatial arrangement for Ca2+ chelation [46]. This enhanced binding affinity enables DOX to effectively compete with Mu for Ca2+ ions, leading to a stronger response signal. Previous research has successfully exploited these differential Ca2+-binding properties among TCs to develop selective detection methods for DOX [38]. While chlortetracycline shows comparable Ca2+ binding strength to DOX due to its chlorine substituent at C-7 position, which enhances electron-withdrawing effects and strengthens metal chelation [46], this potential interference is typically not significant in real applications. This is because chlortetracycline has a limited use in modern medical practice and is less commonly found in environmental samples compared to DOX. Additionally, chlortetracycline tends to be less stable in aqueous solutions, particularly under alkaline conditions used in this detection method, which further reduces its potential for interference.

Although Ca2+-DOX complex formation is known to produce a fluorescent species, this complex does not contribute to the analytical signal in our detection system. This is due to the strategic selection of 450 nm as the excitation wavelength, which is not suitable for exciting the Ca-DOX complex. The Ca-DOX complex typically requires excitation at shorter wavelengths (around 380-390 nm) to exhibit fluorescence [38]. Therefore, at our chosen excitation wavelength of 450 nm, any fluorescence from the Ca-DOX complex is negligible, ensuring that the observed signal enhancement originates solely from the restoration of O-CDs fluorescence following the release of Mu from its Ca2+ complex. This selective excitation strategy enhances the method's specificity by eliminating potential interference from Ca-DOX complex fluorescence.

In addition to the selectivity study, we conducted an anti-interference evaluation of the O-CDs/Ca@Mu system by assessing its response to DOX in the presence of previously tested potential interferants (Fig. S5C and Fig. S5D), including metal cations and other antibiotics. The results confirm that the fluorescence response of the system remains largely unaffected by these substances, demonstrating the robust anti-interference capability of our method. This further supports the high specificity of the O-CDs/Ca@Mu system for DOX detection, ensuring reliable performance even in complex environmental samples.

The mechanistic investigation of the O-CDs/Ca@Mu system can be divided into two key aspects: the quenching mechanism and the competitive binding interaction. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was elucidated through spectral analysis, which revealed significant overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of the Ca@Mu complex and the excitation spectrum of O-CDs, along with partial overlap with the emission spectrum (Fig. 5B). This spectral relationship indicates that the fluorescence quenching predominantly occurs through an inner-filter effect (IFE) mechanism rather than Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [47]. The absence of complete spectral overlap between the Ca@Mu complex absorption and O-CDs emission spectra further confirms the IFE-based quenching mechanism. The interaction between Ca2+ ions and DOX was confirmed through UV-visible spectroscopic analysis. Upon addition of DOX to Ca@Mu complex, the absorption spectrum of DOX exhibits a notable bathochromic shift, indicating the formation of a Ca-DOX complex (Fig. 5C). This observation aligns with previous studies demonstrating that DOX forms stable six-membered chelate rings with Ca2+ ions through coordination via intramolecular oxygen atoms [48,49]. Additionally, the peak of Ca@Mu at 505 considerably quenched due to formation of the new Ca-DOX complex. This chelation chemistry forms the basis for the competitive binding that drives the sensing mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DOX in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The O-CDs/Ca@Mu system was effectively employed for DOX detection in water samples using the standard addition method. The analysis protocol involved first measuring the fluorescence spectra of untreated samples without DOX, followed by measurements after spiking the samples with varying DOX concentrations (in µM). The fluorescence intensity was carefully monitored for each measurement after adding the O-CDs/Ca@Mu system. The analytical results demonstrated excellent recovery rates between 97.8 % and 101.0 %, with relative errors ranging from 1.78% to 2.98% (Table 2 ). These recovery values show high accuracy, and the error percentages are lower compared to similar analytical methods.

Our O-CDs/Ca@Mu fluorometric system offers a linear detection range of 1.0-35.0 μM with a detection limit of 325 nM. While environmental DOX concentrations typically range from low ng/L to μg/L levels (surface waters: 10-500 ng/L; wastewater effluents: 0.1-2 μg/L; hospital wastewaters: 5-15 μg/L), these concentrations are below our method's direct detection capabilities [50]. Therefore, our method is more suitable for monitoring DOX in higher-concentration scenarios such as pharmaceutical manufacturing effluents, concentrated hospital wastewaters, or samples from livestock production facilities where antibiotics are heavily used. For environmental monitoring of typical surface waters or municipal wastewaters, a pre-concentration step would be necessary. This could involve solid-phase extraction or other enrichment techniques to bring environmental samples within our detection range.

To further validate the accuracy of the proposed method, we compared its performance with a standard chromatographic method [51] by analyzing the percentage recovery of DOX in environmental water samples. The results demonstrated no significant differences between the two methods, confirming the reliability of the O-CDs/Ca@Mu system. The comparable recovery rates indicate that our fluorescence-based approach provides accurate quantification of DOX.

In this work, we have successfully developed a novel hybrid sensing system for selective doxycycline detection by combining orange carbon dots (O-CDs) with a Ca@Mu complex. The unique design exploits competitive binding between DOX and murexide for Ca2+ ions, offering significant advantages over conventional CDs-based methods that rely on direct fluorescence quenching. The system demonstrates dual-mode detection capability through simultaneous colorimetric and fluorometric responses, providing complementary analytical signals that enhance detection reliability. The sensing mechanism was comprehensively investigated and confirmed through various spectroscopic techniques, revealing that the initial fluorescence quenching occurs through an inner filter effect, while the selective response to DOX is achieved through preferential Ca2+ binding. The method exhibits excellent analytical performance with good linearity, low detection limit, and most notably, outstanding selectivity for DOX among other tetracyclines and potential interferents commonly present in environmental samples. This high selectivity, particularly the ability to differentiate DOX from structurally similar tetracyclines, addresses a significant limitation of existing detection methods. Furthermore, the successful development of a smartphone-based platform for colorimetric detection extends the method's utility to on-site analysis, making it particularly valuable for environmental monitoring applications. The practical applicability of the method was validated through successful analysis of environmental water samples with excellent recovery rates, demonstrating its reliability in real-world applications. This work not only provides a new analytical tool for DOX detection but also introduces a novel strategy for designing selective sensors based on competitive metal binding. The approach described here could potentially be adapted for the detection of other metal-binding analytes, opening new avenues in environmental and pharmaceutical analysis.
The authors declare that there are no competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have influenced the work presented in this paper.

Fatma A.M. Abdel-aal: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Validation, Software, Resources, Data curation, Conceptualization. Azza H. Rageh: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Mohamed R. Elmasry: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Yousef A. Bin Jardan: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2025.100438.
